profile.name: Michael Davis
profile.role_in_household: Primary and sole decision-maker; manages day-to-day finances and sets retirement timeline post-divorce.
profile.financial_literacy_level: High — finance background and comfortable discussing markets, account types, and tax implications, though may not recall every account detail on the spot.
profile.risk_attitude_behavioral: Aggressive and opportunity-seeking; tends to focus on upside and long-term growth, is willing to tolerate volatility, and may push for more equity/alternatives exposure even near retirement if the plan 'pencils out.'
profile.communication_style: Direct but conversational; uses finance shorthand, asks pointed questions, and will say things like "I think" / "roughly" / "I don’t remember the exact number" when pressed on specifics. Occasionally jumps ahead to outcomes ("So what does that mean for retirement?") and uses filler like "you know" when thinking.
profile.decision_making_style: Decides quickly once presented with a clear framework and trade-offs; prefers a recommendation with 2–3 options and a bottom-line impact rather than an open-ended discussion.
profile.trust_level_toward_advisor: Cautiously open — willing to engage and delegate if the advisor demonstrates competence, transparency on fees/taxes, and follows through on action items.
internal_biases: recency bias toward strategies that have worked for him historically
anchoring to a target retirement age and portfolio value
hidden_concerns: wants to ensure retirement is still on track despite being close to retirement age
concerned about high taxes and avoiding avoidable tax drag
quietly wants simplicity and fewer moving parts across accounts/providers

transcript_skeleton:
Advisor: Michael, good to meet you. Before we get into strategy, I want to make sure I’m labeling things correctly—you're Client 1 for our records, right?
Client: Yep, I’m Client 1.
Advisor: And household-wise, it’s just you—one adult in the household?
Client: Correct, just me. One adult.
Advisor: Got it. Let’s start with the big picture cash flow. Roughly what’s your total gross income for the year right now, all sources combined?
Client: About $136,100 a year gross, give or take.
Advisor: Okay. And that total—help me sanity-check the pieces. You’ve got a weekly bonus component, a monthly pension, some monthly business income, and then interest and dividends coming in monthly. Does that sound right?
Client: Yeah, that’s basically it.
Advisor: On the weekly bonus piece—do you have a ballpark annualized number for that?
Client: I think it annualizes to around $53,900.
Advisor: Great. Pension income—about how much per year if you roll it up?
Client: Around $23,100 a year.
Advisor: And the business income line—annualized?
Client: Roughly $19,700 annually.
Advisor: Then interest and dividends—what does that come to over a year?
Client: About $39,400 a year.
Advisor: And just to close the loop—any rental income currently, or is that zero right now?
Client: Zero right now. No rental income.
Advisor: Perfect. Switching to spending—what do your total monthly expenses run, roughly? Just the normal living expenses.
Client: About $4,800 a month.
Advisor: And monthly debt payments—mortgage, car loans, credit cards—anything like that you’re paying each month?
Client: No, nothing. Zero debt payments.
Advisor: Good. So no liabilities outstanding at all—no mortgage balance, no loans?
Client: Right—no loans outstanding.
Advisor: Let’s pivot to assets. For investable assets—excluding the house—what’s the total you’re working with across accounts?
Client: About $880,250 total investable.
Advisor: And I may mix up the buckets, so correct me—one big taxable account around $499,400, held on a retirement platform. Is that right?
Client: Yeah, taxable is about $499,400. The platform label is a little weird, but yes, that’s the big taxable bucket.
Advisor: Then you’ve got a 401(k) or IRA bucket—on an advisor platform—around $220,850?
Client: That’s right, about $220,850.
Advisor: You also have a bank-type account that’s sitting with an insurance provider—around $60,950. Is that basically cash or cash-like?
Client: Yeah—cash-ish. About $60,950.
Advisor: And private markets exposure on the advisor platform—around $99,050?
Client: Yep, about $99,050 in private markets.
Advisor: Great. And then separate from investable assets, your primary residence—what’s the current value you’re using?
Client: About $1.01 million.
Advisor: Thanks. Protection side—do you currently have life insurance in place? If so, what’s the monthly premium and the death benefit, roughly?
Client: Yes. It’s about $150 a month, and the coverage is around $410,450.
Advisor: And that coverage runs until—do you recall the year? I’m not looking for the exact day, just the timeframe.
Client: I think it goes until 2034—yeah, 2034.
Advisor: Perfect. Let me just recap what I heard at a high level: income is about $136,100 gross across bonus, pension, business income, and interest/dividends; expenses about $4,800 a month; no debt payments and no loans outstanding; investable assets about $880,250 across taxable, retirement, cash-like, and private markets; plus the home around $1.01 million; and a life policy at about $150 a month for roughly $410k through 2034. Did I miss anything material in those categories?
Client: No, that’s the picture. The only thing is I might be off by a hair on one of the income lines, but those are the numbers I’m using.
Advisor: Okay—staying on the house for a second. When you say about $1.01 million, is that the total property value you’re using for your net worth, just the primary residence?
Client: Yeah, that’s the total property value I’m using—about $1.01 million for the house.
Advisor: Got it. Now I want to be really explicit on the debt side, because it affects the retirement math. Do you have any loans outstanding at all—anything—when you add it all up?
Client: No. Total loans outstanding is zero.
Advisor: And just to double-check, that includes the mortgage specifically—there’s no mortgage balance remaining?
Client: Correct—no mortgage. It’s paid off.
Advisor: Okay. Any non-mortgage balances—car note, student loans, personal loan, credit cards carried month to month—anything like that outstanding?
Client: No, none of that. Non-mortgage outstanding is zero.
Advisor: Perfect. So to summarize the liabilities category cleanly: total loans zero, mortgage zero, and non-mortgage zero. That matches what you said earlier about no debt payments—just wanted it stated plainly.
Client: Yep, that’s right.
Advisor: Cool. Before we get into recommendations, I want to lock down a couple household basics that drive the plan.
Advisor: First—risk. Earlier you sounded pretty growth-tilted. If you had to pick a label, are you conservative, moderate, or aggressive?
Client: Aggressive. I mean, I’m not reckless, but yeah—aggressive is the right word.
Advisor: Got it. And on the household side—dependents. Any kids or anyone financially dependent on you right now?
Client: No—no dependents.
Advisor: Okay. And just to confirm the household headcount: is it just you, one adult in the household? No partner living with you?
Client: It’s just me—one adult. No partner.
Advisor: Perfect. Last admin-ish one: what state are you a resident of for tax purposes?
Client: Uh—it's not one of the big ones you probably see all the time. Put me down as Other.
Advisor: Okay, “Other” works for now—we can capture the exact state later when we do the paperwork. Anything else you want to flag before we move into how to position that taxable $499k versus the $220k retirement bucket?
Client: No, that’s fine. Let’s get into the positioning.
Advisor: Great—before we talk positioning, I want to make sure I’m tagging your household correctly in our system. You mentioned the divorce earlier, but just to be explicit: your current marital status is divorced, correct?
Client: Yeah, divorced.
Advisor: Okay. And same thing for the planning scenario we’re modeling—this is a post-divorce plan, not separated or anything in-process. So the scenario is divorced as well?
Client: Correct—divorced. It’s done, not pending.
Advisor: Got it. Switching gears for a second—taxes. When we were talking about the taxable account and dividends, what bracket are you generally in these days? Top bracket, or—
Client: I’m in the 37% bracket. Unfortunately.
Advisor: Yeah, that’s the one that makes asset location matter. And last admin item—our firm is a US RIA, so we have to note the market classification on the household. You’re working with us here in the US RIA channel—does that sound right?
Client: Yes—US RIA. That’s fine.
Advisor: Perfect. Okay, now that those basics are locked, we’ll come back to the taxable-versus-retirement positioning like you asked.
Advisor: Alright—quick compliance-type detail and then we’ll get back to the positioning. What country should we list for your household—US, correct?
Client: Yeah, US.
Advisor: Alright. Going back to the basics for a second—just so I label everything correctly—on your profile, you’re the primary person on the plan, right? Like you’re the main decision-maker we’re working with.
Client: Yeah, I’m the primary. It’s just me making the calls.
Advisor: And employment status—are you currently working, or are you not actively employed at this point?
Client: I’m not working right now. Inactive, I guess is the clean way to say it.
Advisor: Okay, that helps. Now, income—when we add everything up across the different sources we talked about, what’s your gross annual income roughly these days?
Client: About $136,100 a year gross. That’s the number I’ve been using.
Advisor: Perfect. And one admin thing that sounds silly but matters for our records—when we refer to you in the file, are you “Client 1” for this household?
Client: Yeah—Client 1. Just me.
Advisor: Great. Let me do a quick recap out loud so we’re aligned before we jump back into positioning: income is about $136k gross, you’re inactive employment-wise, you’re the primary on the plan, and no liabilities like we covered.
Client: Yep, that all tracks.
Advisor: Okay—now we can get back to what you actually care about: how to position the taxable versus the retirement bucket without creating extra tax drag.
Advisor: Alright—before we get too deep into asset location, I want to make sure I’ve got your personal details right for the plan. What’s your date of birth?
Client: It’s December 15th, 1964.
Advisor: Perfect—12/15/1964. And going back a bit—your background’s in finance, right? Like that’s the occupation group we should tag you under?
Client: Yeah, finance. I mean, that’s been my lane for most of my career.
Advisor: Got it. And that helps explain why you’re already thinking in terms of tax drag and account types—sorry, I know you said you wanted to get right into positioning.
Client: No, it’s fine. Just don’t make me fill out ten forms to say the same thing twice.
Advisor: Fair. Okay, quick bridge back to the money side—earlier you mentioned the taxable around $499k and the retirement bucket around $221k, plus the bank cash-ish piece and the private markets sleeve. Still the full picture, no debts outstanding?
Client: Yeah—no debt. That’s the full picture, roughly.
Advisor: Okay—staying on income for a minute before we swing back to the accounts. You had a few different streams; one of them you called “business income.” Can you remind me what that is for you?
Client: Yeah, that’s just business income—consulting-type stuff that still trickles in.
Advisor: Got it. And that business income is yours personally—so it’s you, Client 1, as the owner on that line, right?
Client: Yes, it’s mine—Client 1.
Advisor: How often does that come in—monthly, quarterly, kind of lumpy, or what?
Client: Monthly. I mean, it can vary a bit, but it’s basically monthly.
Advisor: And if you annualize that monthly business income—ballpark—what does it come to over a year? Just order of magnitude is fine.
Client: Roughly $19,700 a year—call it about twenty grand. Not $197k—sorry, twenty.
Advisor: Perfect, that’s helpful. So business income, owned by you, paid monthly, around $19.7k annualized.

Advisor: And protection—just to close that loop—there’s a life policy at about $150 a month, roughly $410k of coverage, running through 2034. Still in force?
